NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed and complete training plan in the budget section, listing specific courses (e.g., 'Introductory Course in Epidemiology', 'Statistical Computing with R', 'Genomic Medicine Counselling Skills') with exact costs. The training is explicitly sequenced by year (Year 1, Year 2, Year 3), demonstrating a clear timeline. The courses directly align with the project's methodological needs, such as predictive modelling, qualitative research, and genetic data analysis.
criteria.drawbacks: While the list of courses is comprehensive, the explicit link between specific course content and the exact skill acquisition for each project objective is implied through the course titles rather than explicitly described in a narrative training plan section. The evidence is derived from the budget justification rather than a dedicated training narrative.
criteria.rationale: While the list of courses is comprehensive, the explicit link between specific course content and the exact skill acquisition for each project objective is implied through the course titles rather than explicitly described in a narrative training plan section. The evidence is derived from the budget justification rather than a dedicated training narrative.

criteria.name: Suitability to Personal Development and Project Needs
criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

criteria.pros: The training is highly targeted to project tasks: 'Statistical Computing with R' and 'Clinical Prediction Rules' map directly to the predictive modelling component; 'Qualitative Research Methods' maps to the focus group work. The budget explicitly funds these specific courses, and the Application Context confirms supervisors will provide formal training in bioinformatics and statistics, addressing specific development needs.
criteria.drawbacks: The application does not explicitly articulate the applicant's current skill gaps in a narrative format (e.g., 'I lack experience in X, therefore I will take Y'). Instead, the gaps are inferred from the selection of courses and the project requirements. While the mapping is clear, the explicit identification of the gap is missing.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

criteria.pros: The training plan includes specific leadership and career development courses such as 'The King's Fund Emerging Clinical Leaders Course', 'ESDR Future Leaders Academy', and 'Introduction to Implementation Science', which strongly support a trajectory towards research leadership and independent practice. The inclusion of 'Public Engagement' and 'Knowledge into Action' courses indicates a focus on transferable skills beyond the immediate project.
criteria.drawbacks: There is no explicit statement of long-term career goals (e.g., 'I aim to become a Professor of...') within the provided text segments. The connection between the training and future career trajectory is strong but relies on the inference that these specific leadership courses are chosen to support an unstated long-term ambition.
criteria.rationale: There is no explicit statement of long-term career goals (e.g., 'I aim to become a Professor of...') within the provided text segments. The connection between the training and future career trajectory is strong but relies on the inference that these specific leadership courses are chosen to support an unstated long-term ambition.

NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides extensive evidence of the contracting organisation's (King's College London/St John's Institute) and partners' track record. Supervisors are described as world-leading experts with specific achievements: leading national registries (BADBIR, BSTOP), international consortia (EU BIOMAP), and co-directing Health Data Research UK London. Specific outputs like the mySkin platform and guideline committees are detailed.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the track record is exceptional, the text focuses heavily on individual supervisor achievements rather than explicit institutional-level metrics (e.g., total institutional funding volume or specific institutional awards) for ss.1.b, though the individual awards strongly imply institutional success.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the track record is exceptional, the text focuses heavily on individual supervisor achievements rather than explicit institutional-level metrics (e.g., total institutional funding volume or specific institutional awards) for ss.1.b, though the individual awards strongly imply institutional success.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: The application details a robust supervisory team with explicit roles (Primary, Academic, Mentor) and responsibilities. Meeting frequencies are clearly defined (weekly, fortnightly, monthly). There is strong alignment between supervisor expertise (genetics, epidemiology, digital health, statistics) and project needs. Specific training plans (bioinformatics, statistics) and mentorship strategies (career development, troubleshooting) are explicitly described.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the support structure is comprehensively described with specific frequencies and roles.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the support structure is comprehensively described with specific frequencies and roles.

sub_criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly links organisational support to project success, citing access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn, Estonian Biobank) and clinical networks via the supervisors' roles. The organisation's focus on dermatology and genetics aligns perfectly with the applicant's career trajectory. The support is tailored to the NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, with specific mention of the Integrated Academic Training Programme.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While access to datasets and networks is well-evidenced, specific descriptions of physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less detailed compared to the human and data resources, preventing a perfect score for ss.3.a.
sub_criteria.rationale: While access to datasets and networks is well-evidenced, specific descriptions of physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less detailed compared to the human and data resources, preventing a perfect score for ss.3.a.

Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Full Name Professor Sinéad Langan Organisation London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine Email Address sinead.langan@lshtm.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Sinéad Langan is Professor in Clinical Epidemiology and Provided Medical Director at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine. She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. This makes her an ideal mentor for my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship and as I transition to postdoctoral research. Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

criteria.pros: The application details a robust supervisory team with explicit roles (Primary, Academic, Mentor) and responsibilities. Meeting frequencies are clearly defined (weekly, fortnightly, monthly). There is strong alignment between supervisor expertise (genetics, epidemiology, digital health, statistics) and project needs. Specific training plans (bioinformatics, statistics) and mentorship strategies (career development, troubleshooting) are explicitly described.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the support structure is comprehensively described with specific frequencies and roles.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the support structure is comprehensively described with specific frequencies and roles.

criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

criteria.pros: The application explicitly links organisational support to project success, citing access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn, Estonian Biobank) and clinical networks via the supervisors' roles. The organisation's focus on dermatology and genetics aligns perfectly with the applicant's career trajectory. The support is tailored to the NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, with specific mention of the Integrated Academic Training Programme.
criteria.drawbacks: While access to datasets and networks is well-evidenced, specific descriptions of physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less detailed compared to the human and data resources, preventing a perfect score for ss.3.a.
criteria.rationale: While access to datasets and networks is well-evidenced, specific descriptions of physical facilities (e.g., lab space, computing infrastructure) are less detailed compared to the human and data resources, preventing a perfect score for ss.3.a.

Professor Langan has a track record of directly supervising and mentoring clinical academics at doctoral and post-doctoral level. She will provide a unique research perspective, complementary to the support I will receive from my supervisors. We plan to meet on a regular one-to-one basis, through which she will provide support to pursue my academic and clinical aspirations, and during any challenges I may face. I look forward to learning and working with Professor Langan during this Fellowship. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Catherine Smith Organisation GUY'S AND ST THOMAS' NHS FOUNDATION TRUST Email Address catherine.smith@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Catherine Smith is a Consultant Dermatologist, NIHR Senior Provided Investigator and Professor of Dermatology and Therapeutics at King’s College London. She directs the Skin Therapy Research unit and is a world-renowned psoriasis expert, with experience spanning biomarker discovery to interventional studies. She has strong links to the Psoriasis Association, chaired the NICE psoriasis guideline committee and leads the British Association of Dermatologists biologics guideline committee. She sits on the Steering Committees of the national psoriasis registries (BADBIR, BSTOP). She is involved in major national/international psoriasis collaborations, including the COVID-19 psoriasis registries, and is an International Psoriasis Council board member. She is the Integrated Academic Training Programme Lead at St John’s Institute of Dermatology, as well as an NIHR mentor. Professor Smith, Professor Simpson and Dr Mahil have a strong track record of working together in a supervisory team and producing high impact research outputs. Professor Smith will be my primary supervisor for this fellowship. She has been my primary supervisor during my pre-doctoral fellowship and we have established an excellent working relationship. Through her role in the EU BIOMAP consortium, she will support access to key datasets (HUNT, FinnGenn and Estonian Biobank) and networks (the Psoriasis Association, clinical networks) for this fellowship. Her clinical, academic and PPI expertise will be crucial for aligning the digital stratification tool with national guidelines and ensuring clinical relevance. Professor Smith has supervised 7 PhD students to completion, and is currently second/third supervisor for 6 PhD students (including 2 NIHR DRFs). She is currently not supervising any other doctoral students in a primary supervisor capacity. Through her collaborations and networks, she will ensure I have access to relevant career development opportunities during and after this fellowship. She will facilitate the international dissemination of my findings. We will meet weekly throughout the fellowship to discuss progress, troubleshoot emergent challenges/barriers, and ensure my personal training needs are met. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Michael Simpson Organisation King's College London Email Address michael.simpson@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Simpson is the Head of Department of Medical and Molecular Provided Genetics at King’s College London, a renowned centre of excellence in human genetics research. He is an internationally recognised expert in the genetic epidemiology of skin disease, with extensive experience in statistical methods. His work has been instrumental in uncovering the genetic architecture of psoriasis and other dermatological conditions, significantly advancing understanding of the underlying mechanisms of skin diseases. He has substantial experience of developing probabilistic models in academia and also for commercial applications through his former role at Genomics plc where he led an Innovate UK project to develop inference tools for population health management. Professor Simpson will be the second supervisor for this fellowship. He has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil. He has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and in the development of this application. We have established an excellent working relationship. His direct supervision in this project will ensure rigorous, impactful, and clinically relevant exploration of the multi- variable prediction model combining clinical, lifestyle and genetic information. He will provide formal and informal training in bioinformatics skills, statistics and genetics. He is highly experienced in supervising and mentoring early-career researchers and clinical academics. He has extensive experience of supervising PhD students, including 11 to completion. His PhD students have often been clinicians (5 of total completed) and he has frequently managed students with three or more supervisors. He is currently supervising 3 PhD students, for one he is lead supervisor. Professor Simpson’s combined expertise in dermatology genetics, statistical methodologies, and mentorship, positions him as an excellent supervisor for this project. We will meet weekly during lab meetings and fortnightly for one to ones. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Dr Satveer Mahil Organisation King's College London Email Address satveer.mahil@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Satveer Mahil is a Consultant Dermatologist and Reader at King’s Provided Colleg London, with a translational research portfolio focused on using large-scale clinical data and molecular profiling technologies to personalise management strategies to improve outcomes for people with inflammatory skin diseases including psoriasis. Her career to date has followed the NIHR Integrated Academic Training pathway, completing an NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship which led to an MRC funded PhD and subsequently an NIHR Clinical Lectureship. Post-PhD, she was awarded an MRC-NIHR Clinical Academic Research Partnership Award and she currently holds an NIHR Advanced Fellowship. She is co-director of the COVID-19 psoriasis real-world registries PsoProtect and PsoProtectMe, and is a member of British Association of Dermatologists and European Dermatology Forum psoriasis guideline committees. She is director of St John’s DermAcademy, a national dermatology education platform. Dr Mahil is the chief investigator of the mySkin study, a longitudinal cohort study of people with psoriasis exploring determinants of disease onset, progression and treatment outcomes over time, which leverages self-report data collected via the online mySkin platform. She led the co-design of the online mySkin platform and leads an NIHR-funded UK- wide multicentre feasibility trial of personalised biologic treatment strategies for sustaining psoriasis remission, in which mySkin is used to collect longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures and medication use. The mySkin platform will also be leveraged in my proposal. Her expertise in digital health platforms, analysis of real-world data, and mixed methods translational research to enable personalised medicine will be fundamental during all stages of my fellowship. She has supervised 1 PhD student to completion as primary supervisor and is currently primary supervisor to 4 PhD students, including 2 NIHR DRFs. Dr Mahil has supervised me during my pre-doctoral fellowship and I have established an excellent working relationship with her. She will be my fourth supervisor for this fellowship and we will meet every two months to discuss progress. NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF) Full Name Professor Sam Norton Organisation King's College London Email Address sam.norton@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Sam Norton is Professor of Medical Statistics & Applied Provided Health Research at King’s College London, with a research focus on the interplay between mental health and long-term physical health conditions, particularly in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. He has extensive expertise in clinical trials, cohort studies, and prediction modelling, and is a senior statistician with King’s Clinical Trials Unit and the NIHR Research Support Service King’s & Partners, supporting the development and delivery of a range of NIHR funding applications. Professor Norton has close collaborations with Professor Smith and Dr Mahil, with a strong track record of producing high-impact research outputs. Professor Norton has considerable experience in the development of prediction tools. I have worked closely with Professor Norton to design the data analysis plan for the quantitative aspects of this research proposal and have established a strong relationship. He will be my third supervisor for this project and will directly supervise my training on study design and statistical methods to ensure I gain the necessary skills to perform the analyses required in each phase of the project proposal. He is experienced in guiding early-career researchers and currently supports two doctoral-level NIHR fellows, both of whom are involved with using prediction modelling strategies to inform clinical decision making. Professor Norton has supervised 23 doctoral students, of which 15 have completed and 8 are ongoing. We will aim to meet monthly during the fellowship to discuss progress and ensure my training needs are met.

sub_criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides exceptional evidence of co-design. Specific activities (webinar, survey, engagement group) are detailed, and concrete changes to the study design are explicitly linked to patient feedback (e.g., 78% willingness shaped the self-assessment approach, 96% support shaped genetic data inclusion, 90% desire shaped communication focus groups). A formal Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG) is established with defined roles.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive, specific, and directly links lived experience to research design modifications.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive, specific, and directly links lived experience to research design modifications.

evidence.text:
This proposal directly addresses five of the top 20 NIHR James Lind Alliance psoriasis research
priorities related to timely intervention and disease progression, to address significant unmet need.
I have co-developed all stages of my project alongside people with psoriasis, their carers and my patient
collaborator, Helen McAteer (Chief Executive, Psoriasis Association, the UK’s leading psoriasis patient
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
organisation) with diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, geographical location and psoriasis severity through:
• A 30 minute national Psoriasis Association webinar (n=69 attendees; 193 YouTube views)
• A national survey of people with psoriasis (n=73)
• A 1.5 hour in-person engagement group with people with psoriasis and their carers (n=11)
From these activities, four individuals with psoriasis (diverse in age, sex and ethnicity) have expressed
an interest in joining my Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG; total n=6). Additional recruitment will be
via the Psoriasis Association and our established PPI network to ensure diversity in views, background
and experience. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings at key decision points (in-
person

/virtual to maximise inclusivity). To reduce digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and
provide phone-in options. All collaborators will be offered formal training via Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS
Trust and the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. Their input will shape
recruitment, data interpretation and dissemination. They will ensure my research is relevant, inclusive
and equitable.
All participants in my engagement group and survey strongly supported my research question and felt it
was relevant. They emphasised: a) accessing timely intervention for psoriasis is a challenge and b)
personalised strategies based on psoriasis disease burden, co-designed with patients, are preferable to
a generic approach.
This work has shaped my proposal. Participants in my engagement group were enthusiastic about a
digital stratification tool to assess current and predicted psoriasis disease burden. In my survey:
- 78% were willing to provide health information using a digital tool (smartphone app) for personalised
psoriasis management advice, shaping the self-assessment approach using health information on
mySkin in Objective 1. I will leverage the Psori

asis Association and my PLAG to inform diverse and
inclusive recruitment strategies to do this.
- 96% supported the use of genetic data for personalised psoriasis management advice, shaping the
inclusion of genetic information in my model in Objective 2. My PLAG will help to interpret model results.
External validation in diverse datasets will ensure generalisability and mitigate health inequity.
- 90% of people with psoriasis wanted to know their current and future psoriasis disease burden,
shaping the multistakeholder focus groups to understand and optimise this communication through a
digital stratification tool in Objective 3. PLAG members will receive qualitative research training, recruit
diverse focus groups participants (age, ethnicity, location) and interpret findings.
PPI contributors will receive training to co-develop Plain English summaries for multi-media
dissemination, co-author peer-reviewed publications and co-present at national events. PPI contributors
will be reimbursed in line with NIHR guidance. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI
activities.

sub_criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
sub_criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

sub_criteria.pros: The research team demonstrates diversity in expertise (dermatology, epidemiology, genetics, statistics) and gender balance is evident in the supervisor list. The PLAG recruitment strategy explicitly targets diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, and location.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the team composition is diverse in expertise and gender, there is no explicit statement regarding the demographic diversity of the core academic team (e.g., ethnicity, disability) beyond the PLAG recruitment strategy. Task allocation is described generally but not explicitly framed through an equity lens.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the team composition is diverse in expertise and gender, there is no explicit statement regarding the demographic diversity of the core academic team (e.g., ethnicity, disability) beyond the PLAG recruitment strategy. Task allocation is described generally but not explicitly framed through an equity lens.

She is an honorary Consultant Dermatologist at the St John’s Institute of Dermatology at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, providing care for patients with chronic inflammatory skin diseases. She is a world-leading researcher in the field of clinical epidemiology and the use of routinely collected health data. As co- Director of Health Data Research UK London (www.hdruk.ac.uk) and co-founder and co-Chair of the REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely collected Data (RECORD) initiative (www.record-statement.org), Professor Langan brings a unique combination of expertise in dermatology, epidemiology, and advanced health data science. Professor Langan has led a large, collaborative, international, programme of work using the power of electronic medical record and administrative data to provide answers for important research questions related to a range of skin diseases. She also co- leads programmes focusing on understanding gene-environment interactions in eczema and is contributing to programmes on stratified medicine in eczema. sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: Dissemination plans are robust and patient-centered, including co-developed Plain English Summaries, co-authorship, and co-presentation at national events. The link between objectives (timely intervention, equitable access) and anticipated benefits (improved outcomes, patient autonomy) is clear and actionable.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the plan for maximizing public benefit is fully costed and integrated into the project lifecycle.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the plan for maximizing public benefit is fully costed and integrated into the project lifecycle.

evidence.text:
learning approaches. External validation will be
performed in diverse datasets.
Study 3 for Objective 3 (11 – 36 months): This qualitative study will use focus groups with people with
psoriasis to explore attitudes towards communication of psoriasis disease burden. Focus groups with
healthcare professionals will explore attitudes towards patient self-assessment and tool deployment.
Insights will inform a communication interface prototype.
Impact:
A patient-held digital stratification tool for self-assessment of current and predicted psoriasis disease
burden could enable timely intervention, greater patient autonomy and improved outcomes, through
equitable access to care in line with NICE guidelines. This project aligns with NIHR James Lind Alliance
research priorities and the NHS Long Term Plan to support digitally-driven, proactive care.
Dissemination:
Findings will be published in open-access journals and presented at national/international conferences,
with co-developed Plain English Summaries for international multi-media dissemination via our
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
established networks.

results back to
individuals.
Working with People and Communities: This project addresses key research priorities highlighted by
people with psoriasis. People with psoriasis have directly shaped this project. There was encouraging
support for a digital tool to support a personalised treatment approach in psoriasis using health and
genetic information. People with psoriasis and the public will advise on this project through 6-monthly
meetings. They will help plan and analyse the group discussions. They will also ensure this research is
inclusive and relevant to diverse communities.
Impact/Dissemination: This project will support people with psoriasis to get equal access to the right
care at the right time, and improve long-term outcomes. It will allow healthcare professionals to provide
better care, tailored to individual needs to improve patient experience. I will share my findings in
scientific journals and at national/ international conferences. I will work with people with psoriasis to
create easily understood materials for circulation via multiple media platforms.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: Specific demographic groups (age, ethnicity, sex, location) are explicitly mentioned for both PLAG and study recruitment. Targeted strategies include leveraging the Psoriasis Association and diverse datasets (Our Future Health, East London Genes & Health). Barriers like digital exclusion are addressed with phone-in options and practice calls.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While mitigation strategies for digital exclusion are present, a deeper discussion of other potential barriers (e.g., language barriers, specific socioeconomic constraints beyond location) is less detailed.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusion is demonstrated across the entire lifecycle: co-development of the proposal, recruitment strategies, data interpretation (PLAG involvement), and dissemination (co-authorship). Accessibility is considered through hybrid meeting formats and training for PPI contributors.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While plans for ongoing evaluation are implied through biannual meetings, a specific mechanism for formally evaluating the inclusivity of the research process itself (beyond project milestones) is not explicitly detailed.
sub_criteria.rationale: While plans for ongoing evaluation are implied through biannual meetings, a specific mechanism for formally evaluating the inclusivity of the research process itself (beyond project milestones) is not explicitly detailed.

evidence.text:
This proposal directly addresses five of the top 20 NIHR James Lind Alliance psoriasis research
priorities related to timely intervention and disease progression, to address significant unmet need.
I have co-developed all stages of my project alongside people with psoriasis, their carers and my patient
collaborator, Helen McAteer (Chief Executive, Psoriasis Association, the UK’s leading psoriasis patient
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship (DF)
organisation) with diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, geographical location and psoriasis severity through:
• A 30 minute national Psoriasis Association webinar (n=69 attendees; 193 YouTube views)
• A national survey of people with psoriasis (n=73)
• A 1.5 hour in-person engagement group with people with psoriasis and their carers (n=11)
From these activities, four individuals with psoriasis (diverse in age, sex and ethnicity) have expressed
an interest in joining my Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG; total n=6). Additional recruitment will be
via the Psoriasis Association and our established PPI network to ensure diversity in views, background
and experience. The group will meet biannually, with additional meetings at key decision points (in-
person

/virtual to maximise inclusivity). To reduce digital exclusion, I will arrange practice online calls and
provide phone-in options. All collaborators will be offered formal training via Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS
Trust and the NIHR Imperial College and Partners Research Support Service Hub. Their input will shape
recruitment, data interpretation and dissemination. They will ensure my research is relevant, inclusive
and equitable.
All participants in my engagement group and survey strongly supported my research question and felt it
was relevant. They emphasised: a) accessing timely intervention for psoriasis is a challenge and b)
personalised strategies based on psoriasis disease burden, co-designed with patients, are preferable to
a generic approach.
This work has shaped my proposal. Participants in my engagement group were enthusiastic about a
digital stratification tool to assess current and predicted psoriasis disease burden. In my survey:
- 78% were willing to provide health information using a digital tool (smartphone app) for personalised
psoriasis management advice, shaping the self-assessment approach using health information on
mySkin in Objective 1. I will leverage the Psori

asis Association and my PLAG to inform diverse and
inclusive recruitment strategies to do this.
- 96% supported the use of genetic data for personalised psoriasis management advice, shaping the
inclusion of genetic information in my model in Objective 2. My PLAG will help to interpret model results.
External validation in diverse datasets will ensure generalisability and mitigate health inequity.
- 90% of people with psoriasis wanted to know their current and future psoriasis disease burden,
shaping the multistakeholder focus groups to understand and optimise this communication through a
digital stratification tool in Objective 3. PLAG members will receive qualitative research training, recruit
diverse focus groups participants (age, ethnicity, location) and interpret findings.
PPI contributors will receive training to co-develop Plain English summaries for multi-media
dissemination, co-author peer-reviewed publications and co-present at national events. PPI contributors
will be reimbursed in line with NIHR guidance. GRIPP2 guidelines will be used to report on PPI
activities.

sub_criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

sub_criteria.pros: The budget explicitly details costs for PLAG meetings (£4,800), co-facilitation (£600), training (£160), and participant reimbursement for focus groups and validation. Staff time and materials are clearly allocated, and the costs align perfectly with the described inclusion activities.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No drawbacks; the costed plan is comprehensive and fully supports the inclusion strategy.
sub_criteria.rationale: No drawbacks; the costed plan is comprehensive and fully supports the inclusion strategy.

criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

criteria.pros: The application provides exceptional evidence of co-design. Specific activities (webinar, survey, engagement group) are detailed, and concrete changes to the study design are explicitly linked to patient feedback (e.g., 78% willingness shaped the self-assessment approach, 96% support shaped genetic data inclusion, 90% desire shaped communication focus groups). A formal Patient and Lay Advisory Group (PLAG) is established with defined roles.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive, specific, and directly links lived experience to research design modifications.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive, specific, and directly links lived experience to research design modifications.

criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

criteria.pros: The research team demonstrates diversity in expertise (dermatology, epidemiology, genetics, statistics) and gender balance is evident in the supervisor list. The PLAG recruitment strategy explicitly targets diversity in age, sex, ethnicity, and location.
criteria.drawbacks: While the team composition is diverse in expertise and gender, there is no explicit statement regarding the demographic diversity of the core academic team (e.g., ethnicity, disability) beyond the PLAG recruitment strategy. Task allocation is described generally but not explicitly framed through an equity lens.
criteria.rationale: While the team composition is diverse in expertise and gender, there is no explicit statement regarding the demographic diversity of the core academic team (e.g., ethnicity, disability) beyond the PLAG recruitment strategy. Task allocation is described generally but not explicitly framed through an equity lens.
